coredata.dc:title: Incorporating ionic carbon dots in polyamide nanofiltration membranes for high perm-selectivity and antifouling performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Han
Mou, Zihao
Lim, Yu Jie
Liu, Bo
Wang, Rong
Zhang, Wang
Zhou, Kun
coredata.dc:description: Recent advances in nanotechnology have brought great opportunities to the developing next-generation nanofiltration membranes for addressing the concern of global water scarcity and energy crisis. Carbon dots have been deemed as a promising nanomaterial for enhancing the nanofiltration performance of polymeric membranes. Herein, carbon dots with cationic amine groups (PEI-CDs) and those with anionic sulfonate groups (PS-CDs) are incorporated into the polyamide selective layer of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes to create charged nanovoids (free volume) between the carbon dots and the surrounding polyamide network for high-efficiency nanofiltration. The positively charged amine group-based and negatively charged sulfonate group-based nanovoids in the resultant TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes, respectively, provide alternative pathways for efficient water permeation while effectively rejecting divalent ions via the Donnan and dielectric exclusion effects, thereby overcoming the recurring trade-off between permeability and selectivity encountered by dense polymeric membranes. In particular, the creation of negatively charged nanovoids enables the TFN-PS-CDs membrane to achieve one of the best performances among the recently reported nanofiltration membranes, by showing a nearly tripled pure water permeability of 30.9 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 as well as a higher Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.4% than the membrane with a pristine polyamide selective layer. Moreover, the TFN membranes demonstrate greater resistance to foulants with charges of the same sign as the nanovoids. This work provides insights into the design of nanovoids with desired properties in other polymeric membranes for high-efficiency filtration processes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of a) synthesizing positively charged PEI-CDs with an amine group-based shell and negatively charged PS-CDs with a sulfonate group-based shell and b) creating charged nanovoids (free volume at the interface between the carbon dots and surrounding polyamide matrix) in TFN membranes based on the charged carbon dots for enhanced nanofiltration and antifouling performances. Note that the illustration intends to highlight the creation of charged nanovoids in the polyamide matrix, while the sizes of the PEI-CDs, PS-CDs (both <10 nm), and polyamide layer (∼70 nm) in b) are not in their actual proportion.

Characteristics of PEI-CDs and PS-CDs: a) particle size and size distribution revealed by TEM images, b) 1H NMR spectra, c) FTIR spectra (the PEI spectrum is provided for comparison), and d) full-scan XPS spectra. The inset in d) shows the high-resolution S 2p XPS spectrum of the PS-CDs.

Chemical and physical properties of the polyamide layer in the TFC and carbon dots-based TFN membranes: a) FTIR spectra, b) full-scan XPS spectra and deconvoluted S 2p XPS spectrum, and c) MWCO measurement curves and pore size distribution obtained from the results.

Surface charge and hydrophilicity of the polyamide selective layers in the TFC and carbon dots-based TFN membranes: a) zeta potentials and isoelectric points and b) water contact angles determined from the cross-section images of water droplets captured on the polyamide layers.

Surface and cross-section morphologies of the a) TFC, b) TFN-PEI-CDs, and c) TFN-PS-CDs membranes: FE-SEM (the left column) and AFM images (the middle column) of the polyamide layer surfaces; FE-SEM cross-sectional images of the membrane composite structure (the right column). The surface roughness R a and the selective layer thickness d are labelled in the respective images.

Effects of the loading concentrations of the a) PEI-CDs and b) PS-CDs on the salt water permeability and salt rejection of the TFN membranes. The total concentration of the amine group-containing carbon dots and PIP monomers was fixed at 1.0 wt%. Effects of the total amine concentration (PEI-CDs or PS-CDs and PIP) on the salt water permeability rejection of the c) TFN-PEI-CDs and d) TFN-PS-CDs membranes. The concentration ratios of PEI-CDs and PS-CDs to PIP monomers were fixed at 2:8 and 4:6 respectively. Feed solutions of 2000 ppm MgSO4 and Na2SO4 were used for the filtration tests of the a,c) TFN-PEI-CDs and b,d) TFN-PS-CDs membranes, respectively, and the operating pressure is 6 bar. The filtration tests were conducted on three independently fabricated samples of each membrane to obtain the average value and error bar (sample standard deviation, n = 3) for each data point in the figure.

a) Water permeability and b) rejection rates of the TFC and carbon dots-based TFN membranes for treating different salt and nutrient ion solutions. The counterions in the solutions of H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, and NH4 + ions are Na+, Na+, Mg2+, and Cl−, respectively. Salt concentration: 2000 ppm; nutrient ion concentration: 1000 ppm; operating pressure: 6 bar. The filtration tests were conducted on three independently fabricated samples of each membrane to obtain the average value and error bar (sample standard deviation, n = 3) for each data point in the figure.

a) Schematic illustration of creating charged nanovoids by the PEI-CDs and PS-CDs for the improved nanofiltration performances of the corresponding TFN membranes. Comparison of b) the MgSO4 rejection the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane or c) Na2SO4 rejection of the TFN-PS-CDs membrane and their pure water permeability with those of other recently reported polyamide nanofiltration membranes, demonstrating the effectiveness of developed approach (incorporating PEI-CDs and PS-CDs in the selective layer to create charged nanovoids around the carbon dots) in overcoming the permeability–selectivity trade-off. The references indicated in b,c) are listed in the supporting information.

Nanofiltration performance stability of the TFC and TFN-PEI-CDs membrane: a,b) a long-term test (14 days); c,d) different applied pressures (2–10 bar); e,f) different salt concentrations (500–2500 ppm). MgSO4 solutions were used for the tests. The filtration tests were conducted on three independently fabricated samples of each membrane to obtain the average value and error bar (sample standard deviation, n = 3) of each data point in the figure.

Nanofiltration performance of the TFC and TFN-PS-CDs membrane: a,b) a long-term test (14 days); c,d) different applied pressures (2–10 bar); e,f) different salt concentrations (500–2500 ppm). Na2SO4 solutions were used for the tests. The filtration tests were conducted on three independently fabricated samples of each membrane to obtain the average value and error bar (sample standard deviation, n = 3) for each data point in the figure.

Permeate flux ratios of the TFC and TFN membranes during antifouling tests by using different foulants: a) DTAC, b) HTAC, c) BSA, and d) SA. In the first 30 min, a feed solution of 2000 ppm MgSO4 or Na2SO4 was used to obtain a stabilized permeate flux. In the next 4 h, 1000 ppm foulants was added in the feed solution. In the last 30 min, the feed solution was changed to a 2000 ppm MgSO4 or Na2SO4 pure salt solution to record the recovered flux. The antifouling tests were conducted on three independently fabricated samples of each membrane to obtain the average value and error bar (sample standard deviation, n = 3) for each data point in the figure.

Antifouling performances, including the flux decline ratio R FD (%), flux recovery ratio R FR (%), reversible fouling ratio R RF (%), and irreversible fouling ratio R iRF (%), of the TFC and carbon dots-based TFN membranes against different foulants: a) DTAC, b) HTAC, c) BSA, and d) SA. The antifouling tests were conducted on three independently fabricated samples of each membrane to obtain the average value and error bar (sample standard deviation, n = 3) of for each data point in the figure.
Elemental composition and concentration of the PEI-CDs and PS-CDs revealed by XPS.
Surface chemistry and internal pore structure of the TFC and carbon dots-based TFN membranes revealed by the XPS and MWCO measurement.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recent advances in nanotechnology have brought great opportunities to the developing next-generation nanofiltration membranes for addressing the concern of global water scarcity and energy crisis. Carbon dots have been deemed as a promising nanomaterial for enhancing the nanofiltration performance of polymeric membranes. Herein, carbon dots with cationic amine groups (PEI-CDs) and those with anionic sulfonate groups (PS-CDs) are incorporated into the polyamide selective layer of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes to create charged nanovoids (free volume) between the carbon dots and the surrounding polyamide network for high-efficiency nanofiltration. The positively charged amine group-based and negatively charged sulfonate group-based nanovoids in the resultant TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes, respectively, provide alternative pathways for efficient water permeation while effectively rejecting divalent ions via the Donnan and dielectric exclusion effects, thereby overcoming the recurring trade-off between permeability and selectivity encountered by dense polymeric membranes. In particular, the creation of negatively charged nanovoids enables the TFN-PS-CDs membrane to achieve one of the best performances among the recently reported nanofiltration membranes, by showing a nearly tripled pure water permeability of 30.9 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 as well as a higher Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.4% than the membrane with a pristine polyamide selective layer. Moreover, the TFN membranes demonstrate greater resistance to foulants with charges of the same sign as the nanovoids. This work provides insights into the design of nanovoids with desired properties in other polymeric membranes for high-efficiency filtration processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating global crisis of water scarcity promotes the evolution of sustainable water treatment technologies to meet the increasing demand of clean and safe water [1,2]. To date, pressure-driven membrane-based technologies (e.g., reverse osmosis, nanofiltration, ultrafiltration, and microfiltration differentiated by their sieving capability) have taken the dominant role in the treatment of various types of water resources containing contaminants of different sizes [3,4]. In particular, nanofiltration membranes, which are capable of removing solutes with a size larger than 1 nm, have been applied to water softening and extended into food, pharmaceutical, and medicine industries [5,6].

Polyamide-based thin-film composite (TFC) membranes with high selectivity towards divalent ions, facile fabrication process, and excellent operational stability have been recognized as the benchmark for nanofiltration applications [7]. The polyamide selective layer, formed by the classic interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction, allows fast permeation of water molecules while intercepting species based on the steric effect and electrostatic repulsion [8]. However, the nanofiltration performances of conventional TFC membranes are plagued by the permeability–selectivity trade-off, because the transportation of solutes and solvents through the dense polymeric selective layer is based on the solution-diffusion mechanism [9,10]. Polymeric materials with larger free volumes or pore sizes are more permeable to both the diffusing solvent molecules and solute species, thereby leading to higher permeability at the expense of selectivity [11]. Moreover, the pristine polyamide layer of TFC membranes used for the treatment of wastewater containing pollutants such as colloidal particles and macromolecules suffers from severe fouling, which could severely degrade the permeability and selectivity performances [12,13].

Recent advances in nanotechnology have opened new avenues for developing thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes with superior nanofiltration performances [14]. Nanomaterials such as zero-dimensional silver nanoparticles [15], one-dimensional carbon nanotubes [16], two-dimensional graphene and covalent–organic framework nanosheets [17,18], metal–organic framework nanocrystals [19,20], and other biomimetic and synthetic nanochannels [21,22] have been employed as nanofillers to modify the inherent properties (e.g., the pore size, layer thickness, and surface hydrophilicity and charge) of polyamide selective layers for improved water permeability, solute selectivity, and antifouling capability. However, some of these nanomaterials may suffer from poor size or chemical compatibility with the polymeric selective layer, leading to declined membrane permeability (pore blockage of the substrate membrane by the large-size fillers or increased membrane thickness), selectivity (formation of non-selective voids or defects), and antifouling capability (increase in membrane surface roughness) [23]. In addition, thermally and chemically unstable nanomaterials will be degraded/decomposed at elevated temperatures or after prolonged operation and multiple chemical washing, adversely affecting the membrane filtration performances [24].

To address the above-mentioned concerns, carbon dots, which are zero-dimensional nanoparticles comprising a carbon core with a typical size smaller than 10 nm and different surface functional groups, have been applied as nanofillers for developing high-performance TFN membranes because of their remarkable size and chemical compatibility with the thin and dense polymeric selective layer, as well as their high thermal and chemical stability [25,26]. These promising nanofillers can improve the water affinity of membranes, create additional pathways at the nanofiller/polyamide interface, and alter the degree of crosslinking and thickness of the polyamide layer without creating non-selective voids and sacrificing the membrane stability, thereby achieving higher permeability, solute rejection, and/or fouling resistance [27,28].

Compared with those inorganic nanomaterials, the abundant surface functional groups of carbon dots ensure great opportunities for further modification to achieve fit-for-purpose design of TFN membranes [29]. Our research group has previously developed TFN nanofiltration membranes by using amine surfactant-modified carbon dots to alter their size-sieving capability and increase their effective filtration area [30]. Nonetheless, the susceptibility of the crumpled membrane surface to fouling remains a concern. In another work by our group, zwitterion-functionalized carbon dots were embedded into polyamide layers to improve their hydrophilicity and hence the fouling resistance, whereas the rejection of the resultant TFN membrane to divalent salts was marginally improved [31]. Moreover, Yu et al. recently developed TFN membranes based on graphene quantum dots (a subclass of carbon dots with a structure similar to graphene nanosheet but a lateral size smaller than 20 nm [32]) functionalized with polar and non-polar amino acids to achieve higher water permeability, maintained Na2SO4 rejection, and improved self-cleaning properties [33]. Graphene quantum dots grafted with polyethyleneimine (PEI) were used to reduce the membrane thickness to 6.5 nm and increase the pore size for high water permeance and divalent salt rejection [34]. However, few of the reported works considered using functionalized carbon dots to create charged nanovoids (free volume at the interface between the carbon dots and surrounding polyamide matrix) that allow fast water permeation while having the capability to attain higher selectivity/resistance to ions/foulants with charges of the same sign as the nanovoids.

It should be note that the surface charge of graphene quantum dots is uneasy to be modified because most of the functional groups are located at the edge [35]. The conventional method adopted for synthesizing functionalized graphene quantum dots with different charges involves the heat treatment of a mixture of various precursors, making it difficult for efficient charge modification as some of the charged precursors will be anchored on the ionizable surface functional groups [36]. Quasi-spherical carbon quantum dots directly derived from polymers with rich ionizable functional groups could be a potential candidate for subsequent charge modification.

Herein, based on solvothermal treatment of hyperbranched PEI polymers, positively charged PEI-based carbon dots (denoted as PEI-CDs) with a quasi-spherical carbonaceous core and a shell layer of abundant amine surface groups (from the unreacted portion of PEI polymers) were first synthesized and then derived to negatively charged carbon dots (denoted as PS-CDs) by converting the amines in the shell layer of the PEI-CDs to sulfonate groups (Fig. 1 a). These two types of carbon dots with opposite charges were further used to modify the TFC membrane by creating positively and negatively charged nanovoids surrounding the carbon dots in the polyamide selective layer (Fig. 1b). Both types of nanovoids are expected to provide highly permeable pathways for the fast transport of water molecules through the dense selective layer. Meanwhile, we envision that the charged nanovoids effectively hinder the permeation of divalent co-ions (ions with charges of the same sign as the nanovoids) by virtue of the Donnan and dielectric exclusion effects, thereby overcoming the trade-off between water permeability and solute selectivity. Moreover, the nanofiltration stability and antifouling capability of the carbon dots-based TFN membranes were comprehensively assessed and compared against those of the pristine TFC membrane to demonstrate their potential in practical applications.

The polysulfone (PSF) ultrafiltration membranes (molecular weight cutoff MWCO = 20 kDa) were purchased from Guochu Technology (Xiamen), China. Reagents including PEI with a number average molecular weight of 10 kDa (≥99%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), 1,3-propanesultone (PS, 98%), 1,3,5-Benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, 98%), and piperazine (PIP, 99%) for preparing carbon dots and membranes; neutral organic compounds including diethylene glycol (DEG, 99%), poly(ethylene glycol)-200 (PEG-200, BioUltra), PEG-400 (BioUltra), PEG-600 (BioUltra), and PEG-1000 (BioUltra) for determining the MWCO value of membranes; salts including ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, ≥99.5%), sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥99.0%), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, ≥99.0%), sodium phosphate monobasic (NaH2PO4, ≥99.0%), sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4, ≥99.0%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, ≥98%), magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, 99.0%), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, ≥97%), and calcium chloride (CaCl2, ≥96%) for nanofiltration tests; negatively charged model foulants including bovine serum albumin (BSA, ≥96%) and sodium alginate (SA); organic solvents including ethanol (≥99.9%) and n-Hexane (≥99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Singapore. Positively charged model foulants including dodecyltrimethylammonium chloride (DTAC, 99%) and hexadecyltrimethylammonium chloride (HTAC, 97%) were obtained from Macklin, China. Deionized water (18.2 MW cm) for preparing all the aqueous solutions was supplied by an ultrapure water purifier (Milli-Q).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the charged carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The PEI-CDs were synthesized via a facile solvothermal method. Briefly, hyperbranched PEI polymers (2 g) were first dissolved in ethanol (50 mL) under continuous stirring to form a homogeneous mixture, followed by the addition of H2O2 (4 mL, 30 wt%). The mixture was then sealed in a stain-less steel autoclave to undergo a solvothermal reaction at 200 °C for 12 h. The reddish-brown suspension obtained was dried and redispersed in deionized water for the subsequent dialysis treatment for five days to remove all the unreacted precursors and small segments. Finally, the final product (i.e., PEI-CDs) was collected by freezing-drying for further use.

The PS-CDs were synthesized based on a zwitterionization reaction between PS molecules and the amines on the surface of PEI-CDs. Briefly, 2 g of PEI-CDs were re-dispersed in 60 mL of ethanol under continuous stirring at 35 °C, followed by the dropwise addition of 2 g of PS. Afterwards, the dispersion's temperature was increased to 55 °C and held for 6 h to trigger the ring-opening reaction of PS and the subsequent zwitterionization reaction. The reddish-brown precipitate (i.e., PS-CDs) was collected, repeatedly dissolved in deionized water and precipitated in ethanol for preliminary washing, and finally dispersed in deionized water for dialysis to for further purification. The product was freeze-dried for further usage.

The TFN membranes incorporated with charged carbon dots were prepared through an IP reaction. The microporous PSF ultrafiltration substrate cut into a rectangular shape (6 cm × 8 cm) was first fully immersed into an aqueous solution of a certain amount of PEI-CDs or PS-CDs and PIP for 2 min to fully adsorb the nanoparticles and monomers. The concentrations of the carbon dots and PIP were optimized to obtain the best nanofiltration performance (i.e., 0.2 wt% of PEI-CDs and 0.8 wt% of PIP for the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane; 0.4 wt% of PS-CDs and 0.6 wt% of PIP for the TFN-PS-CDs membrane). A home-made air knife was then used to remove the excessive aqueous solution on the active surface of the substrate membrane. Afterwards, the substrate was quickly immersed into a tank of TMC (0.15 wt%) organic solution (n-hexane) to activate the IP reaction and held for 1 min to form a thin and dense carbon dots-containing polyamide layer on top of the substrate surface. The intensive reaction was quenched by immersing the as-prepared TFN membrane into a pure n-hexane solution. Finally, the membrane was cured for 15 min at an elevated temperature of 60 °C to enhance the density of the polyamide selective layer. The TFN membranes incorporated with PEI-CDs and PS-CDs were denoted by TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs, respectively. TFC membranes as the control samples were prepared in an analogous manner (the same TMC concentration, IP process, and curing conditions, but a PIP concentration of 1.0 wt%) without adding the PEI-CDs or PS-CDs in the PIP aqueous solution. All the membranes were kept in deionized water at 4 °C for further usage.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement of PEI-CDs and PS-CDs was conducted on an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance) within a 2θ scanned range of 5°–80° at a scanning step rate of 10° min−1, while Cu-Kα was used as the radiation source. A proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectroscope (Brucker AVII-400 MHz) was used to examine the chemical compositions and structures of the carbon dots with different surface functional groups. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of the carbon dots from a temperature of 80–700 °C at a rate of 5 °C min−1 were recorded on a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA Q500) to evaluate the thermal stability of the carbon dots under inert (Ar) atmosphere. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-1400Plus) images of carbon dots for measuring their size distribution were collected at a voltage of 120 kV. The surface and cross-section morphology of the membrane samples was revealed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, JEOL JSM-7600F) at a voltage of 5 kV. The samples were coated with a thin layer of platinum to enhance their conductivity. The surface roughness values of the polyamide layers were obtained by atomic force microscopy (AFM, Park NX10) with a scanned area of 5 μm × 5 μm and a resolution of 256 pixels. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) for both the carbon dots and membrane samples was conducted on a spectrometer (SHIMADZU IRPrestige 21) within a wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. An AXIS Supra analysis instrument was used to record the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra for evaluating the chemical composition and concentrations of the carbon dots and membranes. Water contact angles of the membrane samples stuck on a glass plate were measured from the water droplet profiles captured on a goniometer (DataPhysics OCA 15 EC) to evaluate their surface hydrophilicity. A Zetasizer instrument (Nano ZS) was used to obtain the zeta potential values of the carbon dots and PEI polymer, while an Anton Paar SurPASS 3 analyzer was employed to measure the surface charge of the membrane samples in a pH range from about 3 to 9.5. All the carbon dots and membrane samples were freeze-dried before the measurements and analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation testing of the nanofiltration membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Membrane separation performance was assessed on a bench-scale filtration setup, which comprises a cross-flow cell (CF042D, Sterlitech, USA) with an effective membrane filtration area of 42 cm2, at a cross-flow velocity of 60 L h−1. Aqueous solutions comprising 2000 ppm inorganic salts (Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, CaCl2, and NaCl) or 1000 ppm nutrient ions (H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, and NH4 +) were prepared as the feed solutions to access the nanofiltration performance. Before the permeate was collected, the filtration system was stabilized for 2 h under 6 bar at 25 °C using the same feed solution for the actual tests.

The pure or salt water permeate flux J p (L m−2 h−1) of the membranes can be obtained by
(1)

where the symbols ΔV (L), A (m2), and Δt (h) represent the permeate volume, effective membrane filtration area, and filtration time, respectively. The pure or salt water permeability WP (L m−2 h−1 bar−1) can be obtained by
(2)
W P =
Δ V

where the symbol ΔP (bar) is the pressure difference across the membrane (i.e., between the feed and permeate side), while the operating pressure was set at 6 bar for all the filtration tests unless otherwise specified.

The solute rejection rate R of the membranes can be calculated by
(3)

where the symbols C f (ppm) and C p (ppm) are the solute concentrations of feed and permeate solutions, respectively. The concentrations of inorganic salts in the solutions were calculated on the basis of their electric conductivity recorded on a conductivity meter (Orion™ Versa Star Pro™, Thermo Scientific). The concentrations of nutrient ions in the solutions were obtained by ion chromatography (IC, Dionex Aquion). For each membrane, the permeability/permeate flux and rejection measurement was conducted on three independently fabricated samples to obtain the average value and standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling testing of the nanofiltration membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
To compare the antifouling capability between the TFC and TFN membranes, both negatively (BSA and SA) and positively charged foulants (DTAC and HTAC) were used as the foulants in the feed solution. All the membranes were first compacted with salt solutions (i.e., 2000 ppm MgSO4/Na2SO4 solution for comparing the TFC and TFN-PEI-CDs/TFN-PS-CDs membranes) for 2 h under 6 bar. The permeate was collected for the next 30 min for recording the initial permeate flux of the membrane J S0 (L m−2 h−1). Then, the membranes were filtered with the same salt solution with an addition of 1000 ppm foulant. The antifouling test was conducted for another 4 h until the continuously decreased permeate flux of the membrane J F + S (L m−2 h−1) due to fouling started to stabilize. Afterwards, the feed solution was discarded and replaced by the same pure salt solution for the next 30-min filtration with the stabilized permeate flux measured as J S1 (L m−2 h−1). Four performance indicators, that is, the flux decline ratio R FD (%), flux recovery ratio R FR (%), reversible (hydraulically removable) fouling ratio R RF (%), and irreversible (hydraulically unremovable) fouling ratio R iRF (%), were introduced to quantitatively evaluate the resistance of the membranes to charged foulants. The corresponding equations are given as follows.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the crosslinking density of the polyamide selective layers
ce:section.ce:para:
The crosslinking density for the TFC and TFN-PEI-CDs membranes can be determined based on the oxygen (O)/nitrogen (N) elemental ratios measured by XPS. The polyamide structure comprises two parts. In the linear parts n, two of the three acyl chloride groups in a TMC monomer are reacted with the amine groups in PIP or PEI-CDs, giving an O/N ratio of 4/2, while in the crosslinking part m, all the three acyl chloride groups form amide bond with the PIP or PEI-CDs, giving an O/N ratio of 3/3. The degree of crosslinking D can thus be derived by

when the O/N ratio is equal to 1, a fully crosslinked polyamide layer is obtained (D = 1), while an O/N ratio of 2 corresponds to a fully uncrosslinked layer (D = 0).

Since the PS-CDs did not participate in the polymerization reaction, the degree of crosslinking for the TFN-PS-CDs was determined by subtracting the concentrations of the O and N elements contributed by the PS-CDs based on the concentrations of the sulfur (S) element measured in the carbon dots and membrane samples [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the MWCO values and pore sizes of the nanofiltration membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the MWCO values, aqueous solutions containing uncharged organic compounds (i.e., DEG, PEG-200, PEG-400, PEG-600, and PEG-1000) with different molecular weights and a solute concentration of 200 ppm were filtered through the TFC and TFN membranes by applying a pressure of 6 bar. All the membranes were compressed under the same pressure for 2 h before the permeate was collected. The solute concentrations were obtained by measuring the total organic carbon (TOC) values (TOC-VCSH, SHIMADZU). The rejection curve of each membrane towards the organic compounds was fitted by an exponential association equation. The MWCO value of each membrane was determined at the point in the curve where the rejection rate equals 90%.

The pore size and pore size distribution of the nanofiltration membranes were obtained based on their MWCO values. The molecular weights of the organic compounds used in the MWCO measurement were first converted to their Stokes radii. Specifically, the Stokes radius of DEG r DEG is given by

The term σ p is the geometric standard deviation, which can be calculated by dividing the Stokes radius at the 84.13% rejection rate by that at the 50% rejection rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the charged carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Two types of carbon dots, including PEI-CDs and PS-CDs, were synthesized for the subsequent modification of pristine polyamide layers. The successful formation of the PEI-CDs from PEI polymers and subsequent modification to obtain the PS-CDs were verified by XRD, TEM, TGA, and 1H NMR analysis. According to the XRD results (Fig. S1), the broad peak centered at 2θ = 21.3° (PEI-CDs) and 21.6° (PS-CDs) indicates the carbonization (but with a low degree) of PEI polymers to form carbon dots after the solvothermal treatment [38]. The TEM images (Fig. 2 a) show the morphology and size distribution of the carbon dots, where the PEI-CDs and PS-CDs nanoparticles exhibit an amorphous structure with an average size of 2.53 and 2.54 nm, respectively, implying the negligible effect of the zwitterionization reaction on the particle size. Such small sizes of PEI-CDs and PS-CDs could also facilitate their dispersion in the polyamide matrix to prevent the creation of nonselective defects due to poor size compatibility.

The TGA measurement was conducted to reveal the core–shell structure of the carbon dots, in which the polymer shell layer with low thermal stability was expected to decompose at a lower temperature than the carbon core. To exclude the effect of moisture adsorbed by the super hydrophilic samples, the data was recorded after the temperature reached 80 °C and being held for 30 min. PEI polymers were completely decomposed at around 370 °C (Fig. S2). In contrast, PEI-CDs and PS-CDs had a retained weight ratio of about 30% at 700 °C, implying that the shell layer takes up about 70% of the carbon dots in terms of weight. Compared with that of the PEI-CDs, the weight loss curve of PS-CDs displays an abrupt drop between 130 and 170 °C, which can be attributed to the destruction of the sulfonate groups [30]. The zwitterionization reaction of the amine groups in the PEI-CDs with the ring-opened PS molecules to form the PS-CDs were further verified by the 1H NMR analysis (Fig. 2b). For PEI-CDs, peaks a–c correspond to the protons held by the carbon atoms adjacent to different amine groups (or amide groups as revealed by the FTIR and XPS analysis), in which peak c is a typical signal for PEI skeletons (Fig. S3), implying that a significant portion of the hyperbranched PEI polymer was retained on the PEI-CDs to form the shell layer [39]. In comparison, new peaks d–g are observed for the PS-CDs, which represent the protons held by carbon atoms at the α-, β-, and γ-position of the sulfonate groups and at the α-position of the quaternary ammonium ions, respectively [40].

The detailed chemical structures and compositions of the carbon dots, which could facilitate the understanding of their possible effect on the IP process and interactions with the polyamide matrix, were further determined by the FTIR and XPS techniques. In Fig. 2c, compared with the PEI polymer (used as a precursor for the synthesis carbon dots), PEI-CDs display a new peak at the wavenumber of 1649 cm−1 originated from CO stretching vibrations [36]. The appearance of oxygen-containing functional groups suggests that the PEI polymer underwent an oxidation reaction with H2O2 to form PEI-CDs. In the spectra of PS-CDs, the two strong peaks at 1148 and 1032 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of SO and S–O bonds in the sulfonate groups [41].

In the full-scan XPS spectra (Fig. 2d), PEI-CDs exhibit three peaks at 531.2, 399.1, and 284.8 eV, which correspond to the O 1s, C 1s, and N 1s signals; while a new S 2p peak at 164.0 eV is detected for PS-CDs, which are attributed to the existence of sulfonate groups. The introduction of sulfonate groups also increases the concentration of oxygen elements in the carbon dots (Table 1 ). Moreover, the deconvoluted high-resolution spectra (Fig. S4 and Fig. 2d inset) enable the identification of the S–O and SO bonds (O 1s spectra), C–S bond (C 1s spectra), and S–O/S–C and SO bonds (S 2p spectra) in PS-CDs. The detailed quantitative results showing the concentrations of the chemical bonds are provided in Table S1. The lower percentage of the N–H bond in PS-CDs than that in PEI-CDs further suggests the conversion of amine groups to sulfonate groups. It should be noted that the analysis depth of XPS is up to 10 nm, which is much larger than the size of one carbon dot nanoparticle. As a result, the elemental composition and concentration detected by XPS originated from both the shell layer and carbon core. Nonetheless, the focus of the current work is on creating charged nanovoids in the selective layer by anionic and cationic carbon dots, while manipulating the shell size and charge intensity of the carbon dots will be considered in future study.

Because both the separation performance and antifouling capability of the TFN nanofiltration membranes rely on the hydrophilicity and charge of the polyamide network comprising the PEI-CDs and PS-CDs [42], the dispersibility of these carbon dots in water and their zeta potentials were evaluated. The images of water dispersion of PEI-CDs and PS-CDs taken under sunlight and ultraviolet light (Fig. S5) demonstrate their excellent hydrophilicity and water stability [43], which also facilitates their homogeneous dispersion in the polyamide matrix (severe agglomeration in the aqueous phase for the subsequent IP reaction could be avoided) [33]. On the other hand, the derived PEI-CDs exhibit a slightly lower positive charge (17.4 mV) than the PEI polymer (22.4 mV) due to the carbonization of a portion of the positively charged PEI skeletons, while the PS-CDs have a negative charge of −9.3 mV attributed to the conversion of the amine groups to anionic sulfonate groups, as shown in Fig. S6.

To simultaneously improve the water permeability of the pristine TFC membrane and its rejection/repulsion towards ions/foulants with different charges, two types of TFN membranes (i.e., the TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes) were prepared based on the synthesized carbon dots (a loading concentration of 0.2 wt% for PEI-CDs and 0.4 wt% for PS-CDs). To understand the physical and chemical properties of the polyamide layer formed after the incorporation of carbon dots, as well as the concentration and distribution of the carbon dots in the polyamide layer, FTIR and XPS analysis were performance on the TFC and TFN membranes. The polymerization reaction between PIP or PIP and TMC to form polyamide selective layers is revealed by showing the characteristic peak of amide groups at 1633 cm−1 (stretching vibrations of the CO bond) and 1363 cm−1 (stretching vibrations of the C–N bond) in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3 a) [44,45]. The spectrum of the TFN-PS-CDs membrane shows two new peaks at 1179 and 1038 cm−1 corresponding to the SO and S–O bonds, respectively, which verify the successful embedment of the PS-CDs in the polyamide layer. The encapsulation of PEI-CDs in the polyamide matrix via amide bonds was confirmed by the peaks of CO and C–N bonds in the FTIR spectrum of the TFN-1.0PEI-CDs membrane prepared by using 1.0 wt% of PEI-CDs and the only amine source for the IP reaction (Fig. S7).

The MWCO values of the membranes were measured by filtrating solutions of different neutral organic compounds to investigate the effects of incorporated carbon dots on the membrane pore size (see Section 2.8 for more details of the experiment). The results suggest that the incorporation of PEI-CDs and PS-CDs results in a looser membrane structure by showing larger MWCO values, wider pores size distribution, and larger effective mean pore sizes (Table 2 and Fig. 3c) of the corresponding TFN membranes than those of the pristine TFC membrane. Although the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane has a slightly lower degree of crosslinking than the TFC membrane (as indicated by the XPS analysis), the major cause of the larger pore size of the former is believed to be the formation of loose PEI-amide networks by the amine groups on the shell layer of the PEI-CDs and the TMC molecules around the carbon core of the carbon dots. After the reactive amine groups in the PEI-based shell layer were converted to unreactive sulfonate groups, the sulfonate group-based shell layer had limited reaction with the TMC molecules. Consequently, larger nanovoids (or free volume) were created between the PS-CDs and the surrounding polyamide network formed by PIP and TMC molecules, resulting in the rightward shifting of the pore size distribution curve and the further increase in the effective mean pore size for the TFN-PS-CDs membrane.

The surface charges of the TFC and TFN membranes are critical to their ion rejection via the Donnan and dielectric exclusion effects, and hence were analyzed by performing the streaming potential measurement in a pH range of 3.0–9.5. The unreacted acyl chlorides in TMC monomers were hydrolyzed to form anionic carboxylic acids, thereby resulting in the negative surface charge of the TFC membrane in the pH neutral region (Fig. 4 a). The incorporation of the cationic PEI-CDs in the polyamide layer resulted in more unreacted amine groups on the polyamide layer surface (because the macromolecular PEI on the surface of PEI-CDs had lower reactivity with TMC than the small PIP molecules [49]), and therefore partially balances the anionic carboxylic acids [50], leading to the increased isoelectric point from pH = 3.7 to pH = 4.6. In contrast, the negatively charged PS-CDs intensify the negative charge of the membrane surface, hence decreasing the isoelectric point to pH = 3.3.

However, it is noted that the extent of the change in the membrane surface charge is limited, which is mainly due to the relatively low loading concentrations of these two ionic carbon dots (0.2 wt% for PEI-CDs and 0.4 wt% for PS-CDs). Take the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane as an example. When the loading concentration of PEI-CDs is further increased to 0.6 and 1.0 wt%, the shift in the surface charge becomes more obvious and the isoelectric point further increased to 5.3 and 7.4, respectively (Fig. S9). A similar pattern was also found in [34,51], where PEI and PEI-modified nanoparticles were as the amine source for the fabrication of the polyamide layer. Meanwhile, minor shifts in the surface charge were obtained by incorporating negatively charged graphene quantum dots in the polyamide layer [36,41]. Although the change in the membrane surface charge by the ionic carbon dots is not that considerable, the charge groups (amine and sulfonate groups) in the free volumes created between the carbon dots and the surrounding polyamide matrix would still greatly facilitate the rejection of dissolved ions, which will be discussed later.

Moreover, the embedment of carbon dots in the polyamide layer affects the hydrophilicity of the membranes, which is crucial to the nanofiltration performance and fouling resistance. In Fig. 4b, the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane demonstrates a lower water contact angle of 34.6° than the TFC membrane (37.1°), which can be ascribed to the lower degree of crosslinking (larger portions of unreacted hydrophilic carboxyl and amine groups); while the TFN-PS-CDs membrane, which comprises additional hydrophilic sulfonate groups originated from the PS-CDs, exhibits significantly improved hydrophilicity (a water contact angle of 23.1°).

The surface morphology and thickness of the polyamide layers were analyzed by SEM and AFM. Small polyamide nodules densely distributed on the polyamide layer of the TFC membrane imply an intensive IP reaction between the PIP and TMC monomers, which results in a relatively large surface roughness value R a and estimated selective layer thickness d of 6.5 nm and 65 nm, respectively (Fig. 5 a). When the PEI-CDs were involved in the reaction, the fast diffusion of the small PIP monomers towards the reaction interface was hindered because of the repulsion between the two positively charged amine reactants. Meanwhile, the macromolecular PEI polymers on the surface of the PEI-CDs react at a slower rate with TMC monomers [51]. The less intensive IP reaction with the participation of PEI-CDs leads to the smoother surface (R a = 5.0 nm) and thinner polyamide layer (d = 49 nm) of the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane (Fig. 5b). The smooth membrane surface also suggests good dispersion of PEI-CDs in the polyamide matrix at a low concentration (0.2 wt%) [27]. In contrast, the involvement of PS-CDs in membrane preparation increased the membrane surface roughness (R a = 8.4 nm), as shown in Fig. 5c. In the aqueous phase, the negatively charged nanoparticles were surrounded by a large number of positively charged PIP monomers via electrostatic attraction and could form PS-CDs/PIP agglomerates at a higher concentration (0.4 wt%) [52]. The agglomerates diffused to the aqueous/organic interface and induced a more intensive IP reaction, and hence resulted in a rougher membrane surface with larger polyamide nodules and a comparable layer thickness (d = 65 nm) [53]. The increased surface roughness also suggests a higher chance of forming nonselective defect due to the agglomeration of PS-CDs in the polyamide matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation performance of the nanofiltration membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimum loading concentrations of carbon dots, a series of TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes were prepared by varying the concentration of PEI-CDs and PS-CDs in the aqueous phase from 0.1 to 1.0 wt%, respectively, with the total concentration of the amine group-containing carbon dots and PIP monomers (hereafter referred to as the total amine concentration) fixed at 1.0 wt%. The nanofiltration performances of the as-prepared TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes was then evaluated by using feed solutions of 2000 ppm MgSO4 and Na2SO4, respectively, because the former with a negatively charged surface and positively charged nanovoids created by cationic PEI-CDs was expected to exhibit high selectivity to salts containing both divalent cations and anions [34], while the latter modified by anionic PS-CDs was predicted to obtain higher rejection to divalent anions.

After the incorporation of a small amount of carbon dots in the polyamide layer (<0.2 wt% for PEI-CDs and <0.4 wt% for PS-CDs), the TFN membranes exhibit simultaneous improvement in the rejection and salt water permeability performances (Fig. 6 a and b). Further addition of carbon dots leads to the continuous increase in salt water permeability, whereas the rejection starts to decline due to the formation of defects in the selective layer, possibly caused by the agglomeration of these charged nanoparticles in the low-density polyamide matrix (the PIP concentration also decreases) [54]. Since the rejection/selectivity performance is more critical to sustainable water treatment processes [55,56], the optimal loading concentrations of PEI-CDs and PS-CDs were determined at 0.2 and 0.4 wt%, respectively (i.e., the concentration ratios of PEI-CDs and PS-CDs to PIP monomers were fixed at 2:8 and 4:6, respectively).

Moreover, the total amine concentration was tuned from 0.5 to 1.5 wt% to further optimize the polymerization reaction. Similarly, feed solutions of 2000 ppm MgSO4 and Na2SO4 were used for the filtration tests conducted on the TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes, respectively. Both membranes suffer from a continuous decrease in the salt water permeability as the total amine concentration increases (Fig. 6c and d), which can be explained by the denser and thicker polyamide selective layer formed [57]. The increased amount of carbon dots and PIP monomers provides additional amine groups to react with the acyl chloride groups of TMC molecules, thereby enhancing the degree of crosslinking and increasing the layer thickness of the membranes. For the same reason, the rejection of the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane towards MgSO4 increases monotonically but at a decreasing rate with the increase in the total amine concentration. However, the TFN-PS-CDs membrane exhibits a slight decrease in the rejection towards Na2SO4 after the concentration exceeds 1.0 wt%. The declined rejection can be ascribed to the fewer unreacted acyl chloride groups in the polyamide layer with a higher degree of crosslinking [58]. The resultant less negative charge of the membrane surface and nanovoids is undesirable for the rejection to the divalent SO4 2− anions due to the weakened Donnan and dielectric exclusion effects. The total amine concentration was therefore fixed at 1.0 wt% by considering the diminishing marginal benefit to the rejection performance.

The nanofiltration performance of the TFN and TFC membranes were compared by using feed solutions of 2000 ppm inorganic salts (i.e., Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, CaCl2, and NaCl), which are commonly seen in different untreated water samples. The sizes of the related ions are listed in Table S2 for reference. The TFN-PS-CDs membrane demonstrates the highest salt water permeability for all filtration tests, followed by the TFN-PEI-CDs and TFC membranes (Fig. 7 a). Moreover, compared with the TFC membrane, the TFN-PS-CDs membrane presents improved rejection performance towards Na2SO4, which comprises the divalent SO4 2− anion; while the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane exhibits higher rejection towards MgSO4, MgCl2, and CaCl2 containing the divalent Mg2+ and Ca2+ cations (Fig. 7b). Particularly, for the treatment of the Na2SO4 solution, the incorporation of PS-CDs in the polyamide selective layer increases the salt water permeability from 8.1 to 20.4 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 (a 152% increase) and the rejection from 97.8% to 99.4%. For the treatment of the MgSO4 solution, the incorporation of PEI-CDs attains a less significant improvement in the salt water permeability from 8.7 to 15.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 (a 76% increase) and a more prominent enhancement in the rejection from 96.5% to 98.3%. The distinction between the rejection performances of the TFN membranes to the divalent and monovalent ions with the same charge brings great opportunities in high-precision solute–solute separation.

To preliminarily demonstrate the possible opportunities of the TFN membranes in more specific applications (e.g., nutrient recovery from municipal and agricultural wastewater containing both inorganic salt ions and nutrient ions [59]), feed solutions of 1000 ppm nutrient ions (i.e., H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, NO3 −, and NH4 +) were used in the filtration test. The permeability performance follows the same order as TFN-PS-CDs > TFN-PEI-CDs > TFC membrane (Fig. 7a). As shown in Fig. 7b, the much lower rejection ratios of the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane to the nitrogen compounds (i.e., NO3 − and NH4 +) than those to other divalent salts (i.e., Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2 and CaCl2) could facilitate nitrogen recovery by retaining the unwanted species at the feed side [60]. The high selectivity of the TFN-PS-CDs membrane to the phosphate ions (i.e., H2PO4 − and HPO4 2−) is desirable for phosphate recovery/fractionation from sewage or digestate [61,62] (while a low phosphate retention is sometimes preferred in other phosphate recovery processes). However, the membranes are far from reaching their optimal performance in nutrient recovery. More effort is required from the material design (e.g., alter the size and charge density of the shell layer) to the membrane fabrication (e.g., control the pore size, surface charge, and dispersion of the carbon dots) to maximize the difference in the selectivity of the membranes to the target ions and other related species for higher recovery efficiency.

The performance breakthroughs of the TFN membranes are primarily attributed to the formation of charged amine group- and sulfonate group-based nanovoids in the polyamide selective layer around the carbon dots. More specifically, in the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane, the PEI shell layer of the PEI-CDs are covalently bonded with TMC to forms a much looser PEI-amide network around the carbon dots in the polyamide matrix [35], as indicated by the FTIR result (Fig. S7) of the PEI-amide membrane prepared with 1.0 wt% of PEI-CDs and its partially retained rejection (but significantly lower than that of the TFC membrane) to MgSO4. Meanwhile, the surface zeta potential of TFN-PEI-CDs membranes in the pH neutral region becomes positive at a high loading concentration of PEI-CDs (Fig. S9), suggesting that the nanovoids created by PEI-CDs are positively charged. In the TFN-PS-CDs membrane, the sulfonate group-based shell layer of the PS-CDs undergoes limited polymerization reaction with TMC, as manifested by the comparable perm-selectivity performance of the TFN membrane prepared with 1.0 wt% (a permeability of 423.1 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and rejection of ∼0% to 2000 ppm Na2SO4) of PS-CDs to that of the substrate ultrafiltration membrane (769.5 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and ∼0%).

As illustrated by Fig. 8 a, these nanovoids surrounding the carbon dots effectively lower the resistance of the dense selective layer to water permeation by creating highly permeable pathways for the fast transportation of water molecules. Although the resultant lager pore sizes of the TFN membranes (0.295 nm for the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane and 0.312 nm for the TFN-PS-CDs membrane) than that of the TFC membrane (0.286 nm) are disadvantageous to the steric hindrance effect, which is an important separation mechanism for nanofiltration process, the charged amine and sulfonate groups in the nanovoids contribute to the Donnan and dielectric exclusion effect for effective ion rejection.

On the one hand, the divalent Mg2+ cation and SO4 2− anion, with charges of the same sign as the amine group- and sulfonate group-based nanovoids, respectively, are effectively rejected by the electrostatic repulsion (i.e., the Donnan exclusion effect). It is noted that the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane with positively charged nanovoids exhibits lower selectivity to MgCl2 and CaCl2 containing divalent cations (but still much higher than that of the other two membranes) than to Na2SO4 containing divalent anions. The reason is that the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane still possesses a negative surface charge in the pH natural region, although with a smaller magnitude, and hence a lower Na2SO4 rejection rate, than those of the TFC and TFN-PS-CDs membranes. Moreover, the smaller hydrated radius of the Ca2+ than that of the Mg2+ ions (Table S2) results in the lower rejection to CaCl2 than to MgCl2. One the other hand, the ionizable groups in the nanovoids could lower their dielectric constants [40], and hence induce a higher energy barrier to solvation of ions into the nanovoids according to the Born model [63]. The synergy between the Donnan and dielectric exclusion effects contributes to the higher selectivity of the TFN membranes with charged nanovoids towards the divalent co-ions. In a similar manner, the TFN membranes can effectively hinder the adsorption and accumulation of foulants with charges of the same sign as the nanovoids, which will be illustrated in the next section.

To further demonstrate the superior nanofiltration performances of the TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes to those of classic commercial membranes and other recently reported nanofiltration membranes, their pure water permeability values, together with the MgSO4 and Na2SO4 rejection, are summarized in Fig. 8b and c, respectively. The TFN membranes developed in this work demonstrate breakthroughs in both the permeability and divalent salt rejection performances, thereby overcoming the permeability–selectivity trade-off. Further modification of the charged nanovoids and polyamide network by regulating the IP reaction and designing the charge intensity and shell size of the carbon dots could achieve higher water permeability and selectivity to different targeted charged species, thereby facilitating the development of more efficient separation processes for selective removal of unwanted solutes and recovery of water and valuable components from municipal wastewater, industrial wastewater, and seawater.

The long-term performance of the TFC and TFN membranes was assessed over a period of 14 days (Fig. 9a and b, Fig. 10 a and b
a,b). After two weeks of operation, the permeate flux of the TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes slightly declined from 70.5 to 104.2 L m−2 h−1 to 69.0 and 101.8 L m−2 h−1, respectively. The slow decreases in the permeate flux (a decrease of about 2% over two weeks of filtration and about 1% during the first day) could be due to the gradual compaction of the composite structure with loose PEI-amide network surrounding the PEI-CDs and free volume surrounding the PS-CDs under pressure [64]. On the other hand, the salt rejection of both TFN membranes was well maintained with only mild fluctuations in a small range of 98.3%–98.5% for the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane and 99.2%–99.5% for the TFN-PS-CDs membrane during the long-term test, suggesting that the carbon dots-containing polyamide layers remained intact after two weeks of operation. The excellent long-term stability of the TFN membranes can be attributed to the high size compatibility between the ultrafine carbon dots and the selective layer, as well as the good interactions between their surface functional groups and surrounding polyamide matrix (e.g., the covalent bond between PEI-CDs and TMC), which could effectively prevent leaching of nanoparticles during long-term operation [29].

The operational stability of the developed TFN membranes were further examined and compared with that of the TFC membrane by varying the operating pressure of the filtration system and the salt concentration of the feed solution. Feed solutions of MgSO4 or Na2SO4 were used in the filtration tests for the comparison between the TFN-PEI-CDs or TFN-PS-CDs membrane and the TFC membrane, respectively. As the operating pressure increases from 2 to 10 bar, a linear increase in the permeate flux is observed for both the TFC and TFN membranes, while the salt rejection is slightly improved as the relatively constant salt flux through the membranes into the permeate will be diluted by the increased water flux (Fig. 9c and d, Fig. 10 c and d) [65]. Moreover, the more rapid increase in the flux through the TFN membranes than that through the TFC membrane is ascribed to the creation of nanovoids around the PEI-CDs and PS-CDs with lower entry resistance [66]. The lower resistance facilitates the faster water permeation through the dense selective layer under a larger operating pressure.

The increase in the salt concentration has an opposite effect on the permeate flux and salt rejection performance of the TFC and TFN membranes (Fig. 9e and f, Fig. 10e and f). On the one hand, the declined permeate flux is primarily due to the intensified concentration polarization effect near the surface of the selective layer (the feed side), which leads to an incremented osmotic pressure (can be deemed as an energy barrier to the water flux from the feed to the permeate side) and hence a lower actual pressure difference across the membrane [67]. On the other hand, the higher salt concentration in the feed solution also induces a charge screening effect as more salt ions are attracted and adsorbed by the membrane surface, polyamide network, and nanovoids with opposite charges. Meanwhile, the Donnan exclusion effect is significantly weakened as the electrolyte concentration increases [68]. The reduced electrostatic repulsion by the membranes to the salt ions results in the deteriorated rejection performance. It is noted that both the permeate flux and salt rejection performance of the TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes are always better than those of the TFC membrane under different operating conditions, demonstrating the superior properties of the carbon dots-modified polyamide membranes for nanofiltration applications.

The antifouling properties of membranes, which are strongly affected by the charge and hydrophilicity of the polyamide layer, are vital to cost reduction in actual water treatment applications by extending the membrane service life [69]. Membranes with charges (the surface layer comprising the polyamide network and nanovoids) similar to those of foulants can effectively deter the surface accumulation of foulants via electrostatic repulsion [70]. Moreover, a hydration layer can be formed on the surface of hydrophilic membranes to increase the energy barrier against the direct contact and adhesion of foulants [71]. Herein, four types of foulants, including DTAC, HTAC (two positively charged surfactants commonly seen in wastewater), BSA (a negatively charged model protein foulant), and SA (a negatively charged model organic foulants) were used for the preliminary demonstration the superior antifouling performance of the TFN-PEI-CDs and TFN-PS-CDs membranes.

Although the TFN-PEI-CDs membrane is only slightly more hydrophilic than the TFC membrane as suggested by the water contact angle analysis and still exhibits negatively charged surface, it suffers from a much smaller degree of flux decline throughout the 4 h of foulant solution filtration and a larger extent of recovery after the feed solution was changed to pure salt solution (Fig. 11 a and b), which can be explained by the enhanced resistance of the positively charged nanovoids to pore blockage. On the other hand, the TFN-PS-CDs membrane demonstrates superior antifouling capacity against anionic foulants to the TFC membrane (Fig. 11c and d), which can be ascribed to both the stronger electrostatic repulsion and more hydrophilic membrane surface.

To quantitatively analyze the resistance and recoverability of the membranes to fouling, four performance indicators including the flux decline ratio R FD (%), flux recovery ratio R FR (%), reversible (hydraulically removable) fouling ratio R RF (%), and irreversible (hydraulically unremovable) fouling ratio R iRF (%) were introduced, and the results are summarized in Fig. 12 . Based on the corresponding equations listed in Section 2.6, a smaller R FD value indicates a lower impact of foulants on the membrane permeability, and a larger R FR value suggests better recoverability of the membrane permeability after hydraulic washing, while smaller R RF and R iRF values refer to less severe accumulation of foulants on the membrane surface. Both the carbon dots-based TFN membranes exhibit smaller R FD, R RF, and R iRF values and a larger R FR value than the TFC membrane, demonstrating the significantly improved antifouling properties by carbon dots, which effectively modified the charge and/or hydrophilicity of the polyamide layer. In actual applications where different types of foulants (e.g., colloid particles, organic foulants, salt precipitates, and microorganisms with different sizes, charges, and hydrophilicity) can exist in the feed stream, the membrane fouling is much more complex, and the antifouling performance requires further investigation.

In this work, positively charged PEI-CDs and negatively charged PS-CDs were synthesized to create nanovoids surrounding the carbon dots in the polyamide selective layer. The charged nanovoids lower the entry barrier to water diffusion while effectively hindering the permeation of divalent co-ions via the Donnan and dielectric exclusion effects, thereby successfully overcoming the permeability–selectivity trade-off of dense polymeric nanofiltration membranes. In particular, the TFN-PS-CDs membrane achieved a 1.9-fold larger pure water permeability of 30.9 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 and a higher Na2SO4 rejection rate of 99.4% than the TFC membrane. Moreover, the TFN membranes demonstrated greater antifouling capacity against foulants with charges of the same sign as the nanovoids created than the TFC membrane by virtue of electrostatic repulsion and/or improved surface hydrophilicity. The structure and properties of carbon dots as nanofillers can be further modified (e.g., introducing other functional groups or using other type of precursors) for the extended application of dense polymeric membranes to other high-precision separation processes. This work will contribute to the design and development of carbon dots-based TFN membranes with charged nanovoids for high-efficiency nanofiltration.

Wang Zhang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing.
